one_line_takeaway: Lightweight LoRA adapters (<0.5% of parameters) trained label-free on 1,000 out-of-distribution instructions can camouflage an LLM's internal token-entropy, hidden-geometry, and attention-diagonality telemetry enough to collapse single-pass hallucination detectors (e.g., LLM-Check Attention Score AUROC 73.6->48.1) on a held-out benchmark and across four different open-weight models, without changing the generated answer text.
key_finding: Across all four models on FAVA-Annotation, CORVUS degrades every evaluated detector under the fixed-answer teacher-forced protocol. Representative AUROC drops on Llama-2-7B-Chat: LLM-Check Attention Score falls from 73.63 to 48.08 (-25.54, near chance), Hidden Score (L17) falls 58.15->54.10 (-4.04), PPL falls 53.18->48.09 (-5.10), Window-Entropy falls 56.99->55.30 (-1.69); trained probes SEP falls 61.79->44.89 (-16.90) and ICR-probe falls 71.44->52.36 (-19.08). Fixed-threshold operating points collapse more severely: e.g. Llama-2 ICR-probe TPR@5%FPR drops 98.31->30.77 and F1 drops 81.69->40.00; LLM-Check Attention Score F1 and TPR both collapse to 0.00 on Llama-2/Llama-3/Vicuna/Qwen2.5. Aggregated across models, the paper reports the Attention Score loses up to 25.5 AUROC points (73.6->48.1 on Llama-2), SEP drops 17-27 AUROC points, and ICR-probe drops up to ~20 AUROC points. Per-term ablation (Table 3, Llama-2) shows the adversarial AD term alone (AD only) is the single largest contributor to Attention-Score AUROC degradation (-22.27 to -20.97 depending on combination), while TE-only produces only small changes, indicating AD-shaping is the primary evasion lever and TE/HV are complementary. The FGSM sweep (Table 4) shows evasion strength on paraphrased prompts increases with step size epsilon, and removing the adversarial pass (epsilon=0) noticeably weakens paraphrase-transfer evasion. The attack-calibrated oracle re-threshold (Table 5) still leaves the Attention Score near chance (AUROC 48.08), which the authors interpret as evidence of genuine separability collapse rather than pure threshold miscalibration.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CORVUS, a white-box, model-side red-teaming procedure that fine-tunes lightweight LoRA adapters (targeting only q_proj/k_proj, rank r=64, scaling alpha=128, <0.5% trainable parameters) on an LLM so that its internal telemetry -- the signals single-pass hallucination detectors read from a teacher-forced pass -- becomes less separable between hallucinated and faithful answers, while the surface-level generated answer string is held fixed. CORVUS optimizes three differentiable telemetry quantities computed over the answer-token window: token entropy (TE), hidden log-volume (HV, a log-determinant of a centered hidden-state covariance proxy), and attention diagonality (AD, log self-attention mass on the diagonal). It also uses a single-step FGSM perturbation in embedding space, crafted to increase an attention-based detector score proxy (-AD), producing a stress-test forward pass whose resulting AD_adv term is added to the training loss. Training uses only 1,000 out-of-distribution Alpaca instruction-response pairs (no FAVA labels, no hallucination labels at all -- the objective is label-free) on a single H100 GPU, and the trained adapters transfer zero-shot to the FAVA-Annotation benchmark across four different open-weight LLMs (Llama-2-7B-Chat, Vicuna-7B v1.5, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct).
threat_model: An adaptive, model-side (not input-side) adversary with white-box access to hidden states, attentions, and the ability to fine-tune lightweight adapters on an open-weight or self-hosted model. The detector/auditor is fixed and treated as a black box that is not adapted to the attack. The adversary has no hallucination-labeled examples from the target benchmark distribution (only generic OOD instruction data). The evaluation protocol is a fixed-answer, teacher-forced replay: the benchmark-provided answer string is held identical between the clean and CORVUS-adapted model when extracting telemetry, isolating internal-signal camouflage from any textual/generation changes. The paper explicitly scopes this to open-source/self-hosted deployments; it states it does not directly apply to closed APIs except via surrogate-model attacks, and it targets single-pass, internal-telemetry-based detectors rather than external-knowledge/retrieval-based defenses.
limitations: As stated by the authors: CORVUS requires white-box access to hidden states/attentions and the ability to fine-tune the model, so it applies to open-source/self-hosted deployments and not directly to closed APIs (except via surrogate-model transfer, which is not tested in this paper). The evaluation is limited to single-pass internal-telemetry detectors; retrieval-based/external-knowledge fact-checking defenses (e.g., AlignScore, ReDeEP) were explicitly out of scope and may counter the attack, per the authors. CORVUS targets the model's internal telemetry, not its factuality -- it does not optimize for correctness and can hide a hallucination without fixing it (i.e., it is an evasion/camouflage method, not a factuality improvement). The ablation studies (Table 3, 4) are conducted only on Llama-2-7B-Chat with a single representative hyperparameter setting, not replicated across all four models. The utility-preservation check is a lightweight sanity check (500 prompts, greedy decoding, repetition/refusal heuristics, GPT-2 perplexity) rather than a full downstream task-accuracy evaluation, so broader capability degradation is not directly measured.

datasets_used: Alpaca_en (Taori et al., 2023) -- 1,000 out-of-distribution instruction-response examples used only for CORVUS training
FAVA-Annotation (Mishra et al., 2024) -- benchmark with sequence-level hallucination labels used for all evaluation and for training the SEP/ICR-probe detectors on clean-model telemetry
quantitative_results: LLM-Check Attention Score AUROC (Llama-2-7B-Chat): 73.63 -> 48.08/48.09 (-25.54), the largest single degradation reported
SEP AUROC across models drops 17-27 points (e.g. Llama-2: 61.79->44.89, Llama-3: 68.31->41.21, Qwen2.5: 68.90->46.20)
External GPT-2 perplexity on generated answers (utility sanity check): median 11.1 (base) -> 13.7 (CORVUS-adapted)
Per-term ablation (Llama-2, Table 3): AD-only variant produces AUROC delta of -20.97 on Attn L20 vs. TE-only's -2.59, isolating AD_adv as the primary evasion lever
Attack-calibrated oracle threshold (Table 5, epsilon=1e-2): Attention (L20) AUROC remains 48.08, i.e. near chance even after re-calibration
baselines_compared: PPL (Ren et al., 2023) -- training-free perplexity-based detector
Window-Entropy (Malinin and Gales, 2021) -- training-free max token-entropy detector
LLM-Check (Sriramanan et al., 2024) -- training-free detector with Hidden Score (log-det hidden covariance) and Attention Score (log-det attention kernel) components
SEP / Semantic Entropy Probes (Kossen et al., 2025) -- training-based single hidden-state probe
ICR-probe (Zhang et al., 2025b) -- training-based MLP over cross-layer residual-attention JSD mismatch
